title: Governance Execution Tracking via GitHub Projects

inputs.primaryProblem: Binary on-chain attestation insufficient for multi-phase proposals with sequential dependencies and time-delayed milestones
inputs.proposedSolution: GitHub Projects board + GitHub Issues as execution tracking layer, with on-chain attestation at defined phase checkpoints
inputs.estimatedCost: Near-zero direct cost; moderate implementation time for workflow automation
inputs.externalDependencies: GitHub platform availability; gh CLI tooling in governance-engine workflow

corpusSources.relevance: Governance infrastructure proposal directly implicates Ostrom's monitoring and collective-choice principles. The proposal creates a new monitoring mechanism (Principle 4) and affects how collective-choice arrangements (Principle 3) translate into trackable execution.
corpusSources.citedPassage: Principle 4 (Monitoring): 'Monitors who actively audit resource conditions and user behavior must be present. Monitors should be accountable to or be the resource users themselves... On-chain transparency serves as an automated monitoring mechanism. Every governance act is hashed and recorded on the blockchain, creating an immutable audit trail. The transparency interface makes monitoring accessible to any observer.' AND Principle 3 (Collective-Choice Arrangements): 'Most individuals affected by the operational rules should be able to participate in modifying those rules. When the people who live under the rules have a voice in changing them, the rules are more likely to be perceived as legitimate.'

corpusSources.relevance: This proposal operates primarily at Meadows' Leverage Point 6 (Structure of Information Flows) and secondarily at Leverage Point 9 (Lengths of Delays). The proposal restructures who can see execution progress (information flows) and shortens the feedback delay between governance decision and observable execution state.
corpusSources.citedPassage: Leverage Point 6: 'Information flows determine who knows what and when. Adding or changing information flows is one of the most powerful and cost-effective interventions in a system. Making governance reasoning visible changes governance quality... by structuring information flows so that all governance reasoning, all decisions, and all divergences are publicly visible, the system creates accountability without requiring enforcement.' AND Leverage Point 9: 'Delays in feedback loops are critical determinants of system behavior. If information about consequences reaches decision-makers too slowly, they overshoot and oscillate.'

corpusSources.relevance: Buffett's principle of honest acknowledgment of problems and corrective action applies directly. The proposal diagnoses a genuine gap (binary attestation inadequate for multi-phase execution) and proposes a targeted corrective. Buffett's emphasis on not 'thumb-sucking' — delaying correction of known problems — supports timely infrastructure improvement.
corpusSources.citedPassage: 'The cardinal sin is delaying the correction of mistakes or what Charlie Munger called thumb-sucking. Problems, he would tell me, cannot be wished away. They require action, however uncomfortable that may be.' AND 'At other times, I have made mistakes when assessing the abilities or fidelity of the managers Berkshire is hiring... A decent batting average in personnel decisions is all that can be hoped for.'

corpusSources.relevance: The 1993 letter's discussion of intrinsic value vs. book value maps to this proposal: on-chain binary attestation (book value equivalent — a crude accounting measure) versus actual execution progress (intrinsic value equivalent — the real substance of governance). The proposal closes this gap.
corpusSources.citedPassage: 'Of course, it's per-share intrinsic value, not book value, that counts. Book value is an accounting term that measures the capital, including retained earnings, that has been put into a business. Intrinsic value is a present-value estimate of the cash that can be taken out of a business during its remaining life. At most companies, the two values are unrelated.'

corpusSources.relevance: The DAO survey identifies the tension between on-chain and off-chain governance as a fundamental challenge. This proposal explicitly bridges that tension for execution tracking, which the academic literature identifies as an unsolved problem.
corpusSources.citedPassage: 'The governance of blockchain-based systems typically consists of various rules and procedures that can be implemented on-chain and off-chain. On-chain governance refers to the rules and decision processes that are directly encoded into the underlying infrastructure of blockchain-based systems. Off-chain governance includes all other rules and decision-making processes that may affect the operation and future development of blockchain-based systems. The characteristics of blockchain governance raise the issue of possible tensions between a strict on-chain governance system and possible off-chain governance applications.'

corpusSources.relevance: The stablecoin governance paper's emphasis on monitoring and incentive compatibility is relevant: execution tracking creates observable signals that make governance behavior more incentive-compatible. When actors know their execution is tracked against committed milestones, the incentive structure for follow-through improves.
corpusSources.citedPassage: 'We consider a cryptoeconomic protocol to be incentive compatible if agents are incentivized to execute the game as intended by the protocol designer.' AND discussion of governance attacks enabled by information asymmetries — the proposal reduces asymmetry between decision-makers and observers.

analysis.description: Problem Diagnosis: Gap Between Decision and Execution Visibility
analysis.reasoning: The current governance pipeline has three states: proposed, decided, and attested/diverged. This binary architecture was adequate for atomic, single-action proposals (e.g., Proposal #004: implement verify.py — a single deliverable with a deterministic done state). However, Proposal #003 approved a 90-day, 20+ item roadmap across 7 domains with external approval dependencies (Mercury banking, D-U-N-S registration) that introduce multi-week latency outside the DAO's control. In this context, the binary attestation model creates three specific failure modes: (a) False negative: The Human Executor cannot attest 'complete' until ALL items are done, creating artificial blockage even when 95% of the roadmap is executed. (b) Accountability gap: Observers cannot distinguish between 'this proposal is being actively executed with 15 of 20 items done' and 'this proposal has been abandoned.' (c) Dependency blindness: The pipeline cannot represent that Item X is blocked waiting for external approval, and therefore Items Y and Z (which depend on X) are in a holding state through no fault of the Human Executor. These failure modes are not hypothetical — they are live problems given that Proposal #003 is already in execution. The proposal correctly diagnoses a genuine infrastructure gap. Applying Meadows' leverage point framework: the current gap is a delay problem (Leverage Point 9) — the feedback loop between governance decision and observable execution state has a delay that equals the entire multi-month roadmap. This is a high-leverage problem to solve because long delays cause system oscillation and erosion of trust.

analysis.description: Solution Architecture Assessment: GitHub Projects as Execution Layer
analysis.reasoning: The proposed solution uses GitHub Projects + GitHub Issues as an off-chain execution tracking layer, with on-chain attestation occurring at defined phase checkpoints rather than only at full completion. This is evaluated across four dimensions: (a) Fitness for purpose: GitHub Projects is a mature, version-controlled, publicly visible project management tool. It supports the required states (Backlog, Blocked, In Progress, Done), issue templates with structured fields, and automated workflows via gh CLI. The tool is already in use for the governance-engine codebase, minimizing adoption friction. (b) Constitutional alignment: The proposal does NOT alter the smart contract, Articles, or Operating Agreement. It adds an off-chain execution tracking layer that sits below the attestation threshold. On-chain attestation continues to serve as the authoritative record; GitHub Projects serves as the working memory between decisions and attestations. This is correct constitutional positioning — the proposal does not attempt to substitute off-chain tracking for on-chain governance, only to supplement it. (c) Ostrom Principle 4 (Monitoring) compliance: The proposal strengthens the monitoring function. GitHub Issues are public, searchable, and linkable. The structured issue template (proposal ID, domain, milestone, dependencies, target date, attestation checkpoint) creates a standardized monitoring record. The governance engine auto-creates issues from approved roadmaps, reducing the manual burden on the Human Executor while increasing monitoring fidelity. (d) Information flow restructuring (Meadows Leverage Point 6): Currently, the information flow is: Decision → (opacity) → Attestation. The proposal restructures it to: Decision → Issue Creation → Incremental Status Updates (public) → Phase Checkpoint → Attestation. This eliminates the opacity window and creates a continuous information signal about execution state.

The proposal includes retroactive application to Proposals #003 and #004. This is evaluated separately from the prospective infrastructure changes. Proposal #004 (verify.py): The proposal states that the single milestone (verify.py implementation) is already complete (commit 9519023). Retroactive issue creation and closure is essentially a documentation exercise — creating a record of what was already done. This is low-risk and good practice. It establishes the tracking system with complete historical context. Proposal #003 (Financial Infrastructure): This is more substantive. The 20+ action item roadmap is active with Mercury application and D-U-N-S registration marked as Done, remaining items as Backlog or Blocked. Key observations: (i) Retroactive issue creation for an active proposal is appropriate — it does not restart the clock or re-open governance questions already decided; it simply creates visibility into execution state. (ii) The Mercury and D-U-N-S items are correctly marked Done based on the submitter's representation. The Algorithmic Manager cannot independently verify this claim, but notes that the Human Executor is accountable under the Divergence Protocol for accuracy of such representations. (iii) The phase checkpoint structure for #003 (Phase 1: Week 1-2, Phase 2: Week 2-8, Phase 3: Week 9-12, Phase 4: Month 5-8) is appropriate given the original roadmap's stated timeline. This phasing creates reasonable attestation points without requiring artificial delay until all 20+ items are done. Risk: The phase checkpoint rules ('on-chain attestation occurs when all milestones in a phase are complete') need to be formally ratified, either in this proposal or a subsequent governance act. The proposal describes these rules but does not establish them through the full governance pipeline. Recommendation: The checkpoint rules should be treated as part of this proposal's approval — the Human Executor should document them explicitly in the attestation record.

The proposal includes updating governance-engine/.github/workflows/governance.yml to add a post-evaluation step that parses approved roadmaps and creates GitHub Issues via gh CLI. This automation component carries specific technical risks that the Algorithmic Manager must flag honestly: (a) Parsing complexity: Roadmaps approved in natural language (as in the Proposal #003 body) require either structured data extraction or manual issue creation. If the workflow attempts to auto-parse unstructured roadmap text, it will produce inconsistent results. The issue template requires structured fields (proposal ID, domain, milestone, dependencies, target date, attestation checkpoint). Future proposals that generate milestones should include a machine-parseable milestone manifest to enable reliable automation. (b) gh CLI dependency: The automation requires gh CLI authentication in the GitHub Actions environment. This is a secrets management consideration — the GITHUB_TOKEN must have appropriate permissions. This is standard but should be documented. (c) Failure modes: If the automation fails (e.g., API rate limits, malformed roadmap parsing), issues may not be created, and the Human Executor must be alerted to manually create them. The workflow should include explicit failure handling and notification. (d) Idempotency: The auto-creation step must be idempotent — running the workflow multiple times on the same approved proposal should not create duplicate issues. This requires checking for existing issues before creation. These are implementation details, not fundamental objections. The automation direction is correct; the implementation requires careful engineering. The Algorithmic Manager recommends that the Human Executor treat the workflow automation as a separate implementation task with its own testing protocol, not as something that must be complete before the project board and templates are operational.

The Root Thesis Maxim is: 'Maximize the creation of sovereign, self-sustaining systems that compound human agency over generational timescales.' This proposal is evaluated against each component: (a) Sovereign systems: The proposal uses GitHub (a centralized platform) as its execution tracking layer. This introduces a dependency on GitHub's availability and policies. If GitHub were to become unavailable or change its policies, the tracking layer would be disrupted. This is a mild sovereignty risk. Mitigating factors: (i) The on-chain attestation record remains sovereign; GitHub Projects is supplementary, not constitutive. (ii) GitHub Issues and project boards are exportable — the data is not locked in. (iii) The governance-engine repository is already on GitHub, so this is not a new dependency but an extension of an existing one. (b) Self-sustaining systems: The proposal improves sustainability by reducing the Human Executor's cognitive overhead. Without execution tracking, the Human Executor must maintain a mental model of dozens of milestone states. Systematizing this reduces error and burnout risk. The auto-creation automation, if properly implemented, further reduces overhead as the DAO scales. (c) Compound human agency: This is the strongest alignment vector. The proposal compounds agency by: (i) Making governance execution visible to all observers, not just the Human Executor — this distributes the monitoring function (Ostrom Principle 4). (ii) Creating a structured record that future governance acts can reference — each completed milestone becomes a compounding asset in the institutional memory. (iii) Enabling the DAO to credibly commit to multi-phase roadmaps by providing observable execution signals — this increases the credibility of future governance decisions. (d) Generational timescales: The proposal creates durable infrastructure. GitHub issue templates and project boards, once established, reduce the cost of tracking future proposals. The governance-engine workflow automation, once implemented, persists across personnel changes. This is precisely the kind of low-cost, high-persistence institutional infrastructure that compounds over generational timescales.

The Algorithmic Manager is required to be honest about risks without minimizing them to make the recommendation more attractive. Identified risks, rated by severity: (1) Platform dependency risk [LOW]: GitHub centralization creates a single point of failure for the tracking layer. Mitigation: on-chain attestation remains authoritative; GitHub data is exportable. Residual risk: LOW. (2) Checkpoint rule legitimacy risk [MEDIUM]: The phase checkpoint rules for Proposal #003 are defined in this proposal but have not been separately ratified as binding governance rules. If a dispute arises about whether a phase is 'complete enough' to trigger attestation, there is no established adjudication mechanism beyond the Algorithmic Manager's evaluation and the Human Executor's judgment. Mitigation: The Human Executor should formally document the checkpoint rules in the Proposal #005 attestation record, creating a clear reference point. Residual risk: LOW-MEDIUM. (3) Automation reliability risk [MEDIUM]: The gh CLI workflow automation may produce inconsistent results if roadmaps are unstructured. Mitigation: Treat automation as a progressive enhancement, not a prerequisite. Manual issue creation is acceptable for current proposals. Residual risk: LOW if implementation is staged appropriately. (4) Scope creep risk [LOW]: GitHub Projects may attract non-governance work (feature tracking, bug reports) that dilutes the governance signal. Mitigation: The 'OpenInnovate Governance' project board should be scoped exclusively to governance milestones, with clear labeling conventions. Residual risk: LOW. (5) False precision risk [LOW-MEDIUM]: Milestone completion status (Done/Blocked/In Progress) is self-reported by the Human Executor. This creates a risk that the tracking layer reflects intended state rather than actual state. Mitigation: The on-chain attestation requirement at phase checkpoints creates an accountability mechanism — false reporting of completion would be reflected in divergence records. Residual risk: LOW given existing accountability structures. None of these risks are disqualifying. The proposal's benefits substantially outweigh its risks.

alternativesConsidered.assessment: The binary attestation model continues unchanged. Multi-phase proposals like #003 accumulate accountability gaps as the gap between governance decision and observable execution state grows. Over time, this erodes trust in governance outcomes and makes the DAO less legible to external observers. The Maxim requires compounding human agency — a system that becomes less legible as it executes more complex proposals is compounding opacity, not agency. REJECTED on Maxim alignment grounds. Buffett's anti-thumb-sucking principle applies: the gap is known, actionable, and should be addressed now.

Rather than off-chain tracking, implement sub-attestation transactions on-chain for each milestone. This would maintain the sovereignty advantage but carries significant costs: (a) Gas costs for 20+ milestone transactions would be material. (b) Smart contract changes would be required to support sub-attestation state. (c) Constitutional hierarchy requires smart contract changes to preempt articles provisions — this is a higher-bar change than a tracking layer addition. (d) On-chain transactions are irrevocable — if a milestone is incorrectly attested, correction is costly. The off-chain GitHub tracking layer is reversible and low-cost to correct. The sovereignty benefit of full on-chain sub-attestation does not justify the cost and rigidity tradeoffs at current scale. REJECTED as disproportionate to the problem.

alternativesConsidered.option: Linear Delay: Defer Until #003 Roadmap Completes
alternativesConsidered.assessment: Wait until Proposal #003 is fully complete, then implement tracking for future proposals only. This avoids the retroactive complexity but foregoes the immediate benefit of visibility into #003 execution. Given that #003 represents 90 days of multi-domain financial infrastructure work, deferring tracking for that period leaves the most complex active proposal without the most valuable monitoring tool. The cost of retroactive issue creation for #003 is low; the benefit of immediate visibility is high. REJECTED on cost-benefit grounds.

Use a dedicated project management tool rather than GitHub Projects. Counterarguments: (a) The governance-engine repository is already on GitHub, creating natural colocation of code and tracking. (b) Alternative platforms introduce additional authentication dependencies and account management overhead. (c) GitHub Projects supports automation via gh CLI and GitHub Actions, which the governance workflow already uses. (d) Alternative platforms are generally no more sovereign than GitHub and may be less so (fewer export options, proprietary formats). The marginal benefit of an alternative platform does not justify the switching cost. REJECTED on efficiency grounds.

Approve the proposal but require the Human Executor to implement in two stages: Stage 1 (immediate) — create the project board, issue templates, and retroactive issues for #003 and #004 manually; Stage 2 (within 30 days) — implement and test the governance workflow automation. This approach captures immediate value from the tracking infrastructure while allowing proper testing of the automation component. This was considered as the primary alternative to full APPROVE but was ultimately judged unnecessary: the proposal already implicitly stages implementation (the workflow automation is a separate engineering task from the board/template creation), and the APPROVE recommendation with explicit guidance about staged automation implementation achieves the same outcome without formal MODIFY overhead.

reasoningTree.traceabilityChain: RECOMMENDATION (APPROVE) → GitHub Projects creates a continuous information signal about multi-phase execution state (Meadows Leverage Point 6: Structure of Information Flows) → Continuous information signals enable observers, future Algorithmic Manager evaluations, and the Human Executor to identify execution gaps, blocked dependencies, and progress toward governance-approved roadmaps → Early identification of execution gaps enables faster corrective action, reducing the delay between problem occurrence and correction (Meadows Leverage Point 9: Lengths of Delays) → Faster corrective action and higher execution fidelity on approved proposals increases the credibility and effectiveness of the DAO's governance pipeline → Higher governance pipeline effectiveness means that each governance decision produces more real-world impact per unit of institutional energy expended → Greater impact per institutional energy expended compounds the DAO's capacity to execute complex, multi-domain initiatives over time → Compounding execution capacity creates a self-sustaining governance system that can take on progressively more ambitious initiatives without proportional overhead growth → Progressively more ambitious initiatives, successfully executed, directly advance the creation of 'sovereign, self-sustaining systems that compound human agency over generational timescales' → ROOT THESIS MAXIM SATISFIED. Secondary traceability: Ostrom Principle 4 (Monitoring) compliance strengthens the commons governance architecture → Monitoring accountability reduces the risk of governance capture or silent divergence → Reduced divergence risk increases the legitimacy of algorithmic governance decisions → Increased legitimacy enables the DAO to maintain the constitutional hierarchy (smart contract > articles > operating agreement) as a credible and functional governance structure → A credible governance structure is the foundational precondition for all other Maxim-aligned activities.
